title: When Semiconductor ASPs Rise Buyers Find Ways To Circumvent The Problem
gist: Summarizing Future Horizons' July 2026 update, the piece argues May 2026's headline 118.4% YoY global semiconductor sales growth is misleading because it is driven almost entirely by AI-linked memory (+320.9%) and logic (+49.2%) ASP inflation rather than broad unit demand, while traditional chip categories stay weak and new DRAM capacity risks reversing the memory price surge.

insights.claim: Global semiconductor sales rose 118.4% year-on-year and 18.1% month-on-month in May 2026, marking the thirty-third consecutive month of annual growth, but Future Horizons argues this headline is misleading because it is driven mainly by higher average selling prices rather than broad unit-demand expansion.
insights.speaker: Future Horizons
insights.so_what: A 33-month growth streak built on price rather than volume is a fragile foundation; investors should distinguish ASP-led headline growth from genuine unit-demand strength when reading industry sales data.
insights.quote: May 2026 global semiconductor sales rose 118.4% year on year and 18.1% month on month, marking the thirty-third consecutive month of annual growth.

insights.claim: Integrated circuits led the May 2026 surge, with memory sales up 320.9% year-on-year and logic sales up 49.2%, driven largely by AI hyperscaler demand for GPUs, processors, and memory.
insights.speaker: Future Horizons
insights.so_what: A 320.9% memory sales spike concentrated in AI-linked demand is a key data point for gauging how much of 2026 semiconductor revenue growth is attributable to AI capex versus the broader economy.
insights.quote: Integrated circuits led the surge, especially memory, up 320.9%, and logic, up 49.2%, largely because of demand from AI hyperscalers for GPUs, processors, and memory.

insights.claim: Traditional semiconductor sectors — discretes, analog, microcontrollers, optoelectronics, and sensors — remain weak amid a subdued global economy, indicating the current boom is highly concentrated rather than broad-based.
insights.speaker: Future Horizons
insights.so_what: Weakness in non-AI chip categories signals the broader industrial/consumer electronics economy is not participating in the AI-driven boom, a useful cross-check against assuming a rising-tide semiconductor cycle.
insights.quote: traditional sectors—including discretes, analog, microcontrollers, optoelectronics, and sensors—remain weak amid a subdued global economy

themes.theme: Oversupply risk to the memory cycle

insights.claim: Future Horizons cautions that every previous semiconductor downturn was ultimately caused by excess supply from overinvestment or weaker demand, and warns that new DRAM capacity coming online could reverse current memory price growth and trigger a downstream correction.
insights.speaker: Future Horizons
insights.so_what: A historically grounded oversupply warning directly counters industry consensus forecasts of two more years of uninterrupted expansion, making it a key downside scenario to monitor via DRAM capacity announcements.
insights.quote: every previous semiconductor downturn ultimately resulted from excess supply, caused either by overinvestment or weaker demand

insights.claim: Despite industry consensus forecasting two more years of uninterrupted expansion, Future Horizons views the current ASP-led, AI-dependent upswing as historically unusual, potentially unsustainable, and vulnerable to correction.
insights.speaker: Future Horizons
insights.so_what: An explicit against-consensus call from a firm with a long semiconductor-cycle track record; a useful hedge/watch signal against overly bullish sell-side extrapolations of the current AI chip cycle.
insights.quote: Future Horizons views the current ASP-led, AI-dependent upswing as historically unusual, potentially unsustainable, and vulnerable to correction
